namespaceDesc: A namespace is a fixed UUID that acts as a root identifier. v3/v5 use namespace + name to generate deterministic UUIDs. Same namespace + same name always produces the same output.
name: Name
uuidDesc: UUID (Universally Unique Identifier) is a 128-bit identifier used to uniquely label information in computer systems. Think of it as a digital fingerprint for data — each UUID is unique and generated without a central authority.
uuidStructureDesc: A UUID is written as 32 hexadecimal digits separated by hyphens into 5 groups: xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx. The version variant is encoded in specific bit positions.

v1.name: v1 — Timestamp + MAC
v1.desc: Combines timestamp and host machine MAC address. Older, predictable, exposes MAC address.
v1.cons: Privacy risk (MAC exposed), not globally unique across machines without coordination

v3.name: v3 — Namespace + MD5
v3.desc: Generates deterministic UUIDs using MD5 hash of namespace + name. Same inputs always produce the same output.
v3.cons: MD5 is cryptographically weak, susceptible to collisions

v4.name: v4 — Random
v4.desc: Uses cryptographically secure random numbers. Highest uniqueness, no structure or predictability.
v4.pros: Maximum randomness, privacy-friendly (no timestamps or MAC)

v5.name: v5 — Namespace + SHA-1
v5.desc: Like v3 but uses SHA-1 hash. More secure than MD5, still deterministic.
v5.cons: SHA-1 has known weaknesses, collisions theoretically possible

v7.name: v7 — Unix Epoch + Random
v7.desc: Embeds Unix timestamp in the most significant bits, followed by random data. Sortable and unique.
v7.pros: Time-sortable, index-friendly, good database performance, privacy-safe
v7.cons: Not reproducible, slightly newer (RFC 9562), limited tool support

descriptions.aeoDefinition: UUID Generator is a free online tool for generating v1, v3, v4, v5, and v7 UUIDs in bulk. Conforms to RFC 4122/9562. Runs entirely in your browser.
descriptions.whatIsP1: UUID (Universally Unique Identifier) is a 128-bit identifier standard defined in RFC 4122 and RFC 9562. This tool generates UUID v4 (random) and v7 (time-ordered) in bulk. Use generated IDs as [Password](/password) seeds, encode them in [QR codes](/qrcode), or verify uniqueness with [Hashing](/hashing).
descriptions.whatIsP2: UUIDs are widely used as database primary keys, distributed system identifiers, and session tokens. With 2^122 possible values, collision probability is negligible.
descriptions.useCasesP1: Database primary keys — UUIDs avoid ID collisions in distributed databases without requiring a central coordinator.
descriptions.useCasesP2: Distributed system identifiers — generate unique IDs across multiple services, regions, or data centers with zero coordination.
descriptions.useCasesP3: Session tokens and request tracing — use UUIDs as correlation IDs that are guaranteed unique across your entire infrastructure.
descriptions.step1Desc: Choose v4 (random, general-purpose), v7 (time-ordered, database-friendly), v3/v5 (deterministic from namespace + name), or v1 (timestamp + MAC).
descriptions.step2Desc: Adjust the slider to generate up to 100 UUIDs at once. Choose standard, no-hyphens, or braces format, and toggle uppercase if needed.
descriptions.step3Desc: Click the copy button for a single UUID, or download all generated UUIDs as a .txt file for bulk use.
descriptions.faq1Q: How many UUIDs can I generate at once?
descriptions.faq1A: You can generate up to 1,000 UUIDs in a single batch. All generation happens in your browser using crypto.getRandomValues().
descriptions.faq2Q: Should I use UUID v4 or v7?
descriptions.faq2A: Use v4 for general-purpose random IDs. Use v7 for database primary keys or when time-ordering matters — v7 includes a millisecond timestamp prefix for sortable uniqueness.
descriptions.faq3Q: Can I generate the same UUID every time?
descriptions.faq3A: Yes. Use v3 (MD5) or v5 (SHA-1) with the same namespace and name input — they always produce the same deterministic UUID. This is useful for generating consistent identifiers from known inputs like URLs or domain names.
